red_lines: Italian territorial losses in Africa or Mediterranean
German domination of Italian foreign policy
Challenges to Fascist Party supremacy domestically
core_narratives: Revival of the Roman Empire and Mediterranean dominance
Fascism as the superior third way between capitalism and communism
Italian martial glory and national rebirth
Mare Nostrum - the Mediterranean as Italy's sea

decision_history.event_description: France defeated and Britain appearing weakened in summer 1940
decision_history.decision_taken: Declared war on France and Britain expecting German victory to deliver spoils
decision_history.outcome: Italian military proved catastrophically unprepared; humiliating defeats in Greece and North Africa
context_factors: fear of being left out of new European order

decision_history.event_description: Italian invasion of Greece stalled and was pushed back into Albania
decision_history.decision_taken: Launched invasion of Greece without consulting Germany, seeking independent military prestige
decision_history.outcome: Greek counterattack humiliated Italy; required German intervention; delayed Barbarossa
context_factors: desire to prove Italian military prowess independently

decision_history.event_description: Allied invasion of Sicily and bombing of Rome in July 1943
decision_history.decision_taken: Failed to mount effective defense; Grand Council of Fascism voted to remove him from power
decision_history.outcome: Arrested by King Victor Emmanuel III; Fascist regime collapsed; Italy sought armistice
context_factors: complete military collapse in North Africa
loss of confidence from own Grand Council

cultural_context.domestic_power_base: Fascist Party, industrialists, Lateran Pact with Vatican, military officer corps (wavering), King Victor Emmanuel III (conditional)

military_posture.force_disposition: poorly equipped forces spread across multiple theaters; dependent on German support

recent_events: Industrial strikes in northern Italy March 1943
